title: 38_promise_all_fail_fast.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: One 404 Wipes Out the Entire Dashboard

A dashboard loads three sections in parallel: user profile, recent posts, and friend suggestions. The friend suggestions endpoint returns `404` for new users who have no friends yet. Because the code uses `Promise.all`, the one 404 rejects the entire group — and the dashboard shows nothing instead of rendering the two sections that succeeded.

Fix the fetch so successful sections always render, even when one endpoint fails.
traceback:
userId = 99 (new user, no friends):
  GET /api/users/99         200  profile loaded
  GET /api/users/99/posts   200  posts loaded
  GET /api/users/99/friends 404  no friends yet

Promise.all rejects as soon as any promise rejects.
The profile and posts data are discarded.
loadDashboard throws: 'Not Found'
Dashboard shows error screen — no content at all.
correctOutput:
userId = 99 (new user, no friends):
  GET /api/users/99         200  profile loaded
  GET /api/users/99/posts   200  posts loaded
  GET /api/users/99/friends 404  rejected (handled)

Dashboard renders:
  Profile: { name: 'Alice' }
  Posts: [{ id: 1, title: 'First post' }]
  Friends: null  (gracefully absent)

options.label: Use `Promise.allSettled` and extract successful values
options.rationale: `Promise.allSettled` never rejects — it always resolves with an array of result objects, each with a `status` of `'fulfilled'` or `'rejected'`. This lets you handle partial failures: render what succeeded and show empty/fallback states for what failed. Compare to `Promise.all` which rejects immediately on the first failure and discards all other results.

options.label: Wrap each fetch in its own try/catch to return null on failure
options.resultLog:
userId = 99:
  profile = { name: 'Alice' }  (200)
  posts   = [{...}]            (200)
  friends = null               (404 caught, returns null)

Correct output — dashboard renders profile and posts.

But: the bare `catch` in `safeFetch` also silences real errors
(network failure, 500 server crash). Consider checking `r.ok`
inside safeFetch to distinguish 404 from genuine failures.
options.rationale: Wrapping each fetch in `try/catch` and returning `null` on failure works. However, it silently swallows all errors — including real server errors (500, network failure) that the caller might want to handle differently. `Promise.allSettled` is more transparent: each result carries `status` and `reason`, so the caller can inspect what failed and why.

options.label: Fetch sequentially with `await` so a failure stops early
options.resultLog:
userId = 99:
  profile fetched  (300ms)
  posts fetched    (400ms after profile)
  friends → 404   (throws)

Still throws on friends — profile and posts are loaded but not returned.

Also: sequential fetches take 300+400+300 = 1000ms instead of
max(300, 400, 300) = 400ms in parallel. Slower AND still broken.
options.rationale: Sequential `await` does not fix the error propagation problem — if `friends` throws, the function still throws and `profile`/`posts` data is lost. It also eliminates the performance benefit of parallelism: 3 sequential requests take 3× as long as 3 parallel ones. This is worse on both dimensions.

hints: `Promise.all` rejects as soon as any single promise rejects — even if the others succeeded. For a dashboard where partial data is acceptable, look up `Promise.allSettled` — it waits for all promises and never rejects.

`Promise.allSettled` returns an array of result objects. Each has `status: 'fulfilled'` or `status: 'rejected'`. Check `result.status === 'fulfilled'` to read `result.value`, or provide a fallback when `status === 'rejected'`.
recruiterReview: Correct. `Promise.all` fail-fast is a real production bug — a low-priority endpoint (friend suggestions, ads, recommendations) fails and wipes out the entire page because it shares a `Promise.all` with critical data. The sequential-await distractor is slower AND still broken — the double-downgrade catches developers who reach for sequential as a 'safe' fallback. The try/catch-wrapper distractor works but silences error information. `Promise.allSettled` is the canonical tool. ✅
